data.title: Organon: What The Buyout Rumors Mean For The Stock
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Organon's shares (OGN) skyrocketed by 30% after news of a possible takeover of the company. Although the market reacted positively, it still evaluates the probability of a deal as just a realistic rather than a baseline scenario. This is confirmed by the fact that even after a strong jump in price, the stock is still trading at a discount to the implied buyout valuation. I recommended the Hold rating in the last article, as I did not see any serious drivers for business reassessment. Now the driver has appeared, but it's more of an M&A catalyst rather than fundamental business improvements. The probability of a deal failure is high, but there are no specifics about its parameters at the moment and none of the parties have confirmed their interest. If the deal is implemented, the upside does exist, but it is more likely a speculative growth on the M&A deal. I keep the Hold rating because the business situation has not fundamentally changed and there is still a lot of uncertainty about a potential deal.

What Is Actually Known About the Buyout Talks

I recommend reading my first three articles about Organon, among other things. There, in addition to financial analysis of the reports, I also deeply analyzed the company's business model, described its development strategy, the strengths and weaknesses of this strategy, and the key growth drivers and risks inherent in the company. This will help you gain a deeper understanding of the business and better understand the reasons for the company's current financial situation. And now I'll move on to a potential deal.

On April 24, the media published news about the possible takeover of Organon by the Indian pharmaceutical giant Sun Pharmaceutical Industries. After the news was published, Organon's shares skyrocketed by 30%. However, the deal has not been confirmed by either party at the moment. The Economic Times writes that SunPharma has submitted a reported binding offer to buy Organon for $13 billion. Moreover, according to media reports, the Indian giant is not even the only interested party. The German Grunenthal and the Swedish private equity fund EQT are mentioned as potentially interested in the takeover of Organon companies. This is, in fact, all the information currently available. There are still many unanswered questions – the specific terms of the deal are not known, the terms of the deal are unknown, we don't even know if SunPharma has actually made an offer and whether the Organon board of directors is ready to approve the deal.

Despite this, I would like to reflect on the probability of this transaction, the valuation, and what investors should do in such a situation. The first thing I want to note is that I fully understand the interest of other companies in Organon. Despite its rather high debt burden, Organon was trading at huge discounts on multiples both to the sector and to its historical averages. I wrote about this back in February, and a week before the news was released, the shares fell even more - by almost 12%. Roughly speaking, the buyer gets for cheap a large company with a ready-made global sales infrastructure, a strong presence in the international market (especially in developing countries) and a good portfolio of brands that generate stable cash flow. For Indian SunPharma, which is actively doing business in emerging markets, it is very interesting to buy Organon at such a price, 80% of whose revenue is generated outside the United States. The synergy in the form of revenue growth and cost optimization seems quite realistic. Therefore, I consider the interest in the company to be well-founded, and the likelihood that the process is actually being considered seems significant to me. Especially considering that this is not the first wave of rumors, and there has also been news about the purchase of the company for $12 billion before. Organon stood out from Merck, but has not yet coped with the high debt burden and stagnating revenues, so selling the business seems like a logical way out of a difficult situation.

Breaking Down the $13B Valuation

In this chapter, I want to comment on the business assessment announced in the news. It was said that Organon wants to be acquired for $ 13 billion. Most likely, we are talking about Enterprise Value, since the company's capitalization is 6 times lower. After a sharp jump of 30%, the capital structure looks like this:

When estimating the entire company, including debt and excluding cache (I took the data at the end of Q4) at $12-13 billion, the equity value is about $3.77-4.77 billion, and the share price is in the range of $14.5-18.3 with an upside to the current price of 28-62%. This is a rough estimate depending on final terms and net debt. The fact that EV has still not reached $13 billion suggests that the market, although it believes in the realism of the deal, is still in doubt due to the lack of any specifics. Therefore, such a discount remains.

The important question is, how profitable is the $13 billion valuation for investors and for Sun Pharmaceutical? At the moment, the company is trading at large discounts to the sector medians and its five-year averages for the main multiples (forward P/E and EV/EBITDA). The discount on FWD EV/EBIDTA reaches almost 50% to the sector, and on FWD P/E in general almost 80%. If we assume that Organon will indeed be bought for $13 billion, then the company's EV will grow by about 16-17%, and the FWD EV/EBITDA multiplier is 6.7–6.9x. In this case, the discount to peers will be reduced to only 40-45%, but a premium to its five-year average of 3-5% will be formed. The situation will be similar with P/E. If the share price rises to $14.5-18.3, the multiplier will rise to 5.0-5.2x. Considering that the median for the sector is about 17x, the discount will decrease, but it will be quite high. Considering that the average level of this multiplier at Organon was about 4.35x, investors actually receive a premium of 15-20% relative to historical averages.

Organon has always traded at discounts to peers, albeit often unreasonably. Therefore, investors should not worry about discounts. Rather, for shareholders, such a price looks like a sale at a price close to fair value and even with a small premium, relative to historical averages. SunPharma, of course, also buys on fairly attractive terms from the point of view of multiples.

If the Deal Fails: A Return to Fundamentals

I see three scenarios for the development of events. The first scenario is that the transaction is successful and at the price indicated in the media. In this case, the shares will rise by another 30-60% to the implied buyout value. The second scenario is that the deal is canceled. In this case, the shares will return to their previous values. The third scenario is that the deal goes through, but at a lower price. It all depends on market expectations. If the final price is still higher than the current one, then the shares will rise, but weaker than expected. Of the three scenarios, the greatest risks for investors are associated with the second scenario, since in this case the market will return to assessing the fundamental indicators of the business. Therefore, next I want to take a closer look at this particular scenario.

In recent quarters, the company has demonstrated weak financial results. The fourth quarter was much worse than expected in terms of both EPS and revenue. I reviewed the report in detail in the last article, so I won't write about it in detail.

The main problem of the company is its low growth rates. Revenue from the key segment of Women's Health is falling, and in Q4 it dropped by 15% yoy. Demand for Nexplanon's key product remains weak, and other products are unable to mitigate the drop in total segment revenue. The Established Brands segment is also losing revenue from quarter to quarter, as demand for these older drugs has been waning for a long time. Only the Biosimilars segment is growing at a good pace due to the Hadlima drug, whose revenue increased by 60% over the year. However, the revenue share of this segment is very small across the company and amounts to only 11%. Therefore, the growth in this segment only partially compensates for the decline in the other two segments.

Although Organon remains one of the best among peers in terms of profitability, stagnating revenues and rising costs lead to a decline in business margins from year to year. The gross margin is negatively affected by cost increases, the operating margin is further affected by the growth of SG&A and R&D, and net income is also affected by high interest costs due to the high debt burden. Management is still unable to cope with this state of affairs.

The high debt burden remains one of the company's most serious problems. At the end of 2025, the Debt/EBITDA ratio from 2024 decreased from 5.54x to 5.39x, and Net Debt/EBITDA from 5.12x to 4.92x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by 2.6 times. The Unlevered FCF is decreasing from year to year and by the end of 2025 amounted to only $505.1 million, which is 43% yoy lower than in 2024. Servicing debt and investing in business growth at the same time is getting harder and harder. There is no question of paying dividends to shareholders or holding buybacks.

Forecasts for 2026 are, frankly, negative - in fact, management is signaling that, at best, we can expect a repeat of the poor results of 2025 from 2026. The consensus forecasts are also quite conservative and no positive changes are expected. The market expects revenue to fall in Q1 by 1.13% y/y to $1.50 billion and EPS to fall by 24.32% y/y to $0.77. For the year, consensus expects revenue to fall by 0.88% y/y to $6.16 billion and EPS by 3.45% y/y to $5.73. In fact the market expects even weaker results than management. The Q1 report will be released in early May, and if the data comes out worse than expected, it is likely that stocks will fall in price in the short term.

Thus, if the deal is disrupted, the shares are likely to return to previous levels and the company's prospects will be assessed from the fundamental position of the company. In such a situation, the further growth of quotations will depend on how successfully management copes with the current problems.

Risks

To the previous risks associated with the Organon business, now another one has been added – the risk of a deal failure. If it turns out that these were all just rumors and there is no real interest in buying Organon, then the shares will plummet in price by 30%. There are several other risks surrounding the deal - the assessment may turn out to be worse than investors' expectations, the transaction process may be delayed, and regulators may impose restrictions. The remaining risks are related to revenue stagnation, weak demand for core products, problems with updating the product line, falling margins, high debt burden and high competition in the market.

Final Thoughts

I assess the high probability of Organon's takeover, as it may turn out to be beneficial for both the buyer, investors, and management, who are still unable to cope with the challenges that arise. A lot will depend on the transaction price. However, the deal has not been confirmed by either party at the moment and much remains unknown. Therefore, the shares are traded at a discount to the implied buyout value. Although the market believes in the realism of the deal, it is still in doubt due to the lack of any specifics. At the moment, I prefer to Hold and wait for new news.

data.title: BlackBerry: The Turnaround Is Here (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Last December during Q3 earnings, I viewed BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) through a skeptical lens. The stock was suffocating underneath CEO's John Giamatteo's vision. BlackBerry appeared to be a value trap; disconnected from its investors and unable to create accretive value in its stock. However, the trajectory has shifted following the fiscal 2026 year-end results and several strategic wins.

CEO John Giamatteo, who I previously criticized for being perhaps too operational and lacking a "visionary spark," has proven that operational discipline was exactly what the company required. By pruning underperforming assets and doubling down on core competencies—specifically secure connectivity and safety-certified operating systems—BlackBerry is finally showing some life. With a clean balance sheet in sight and an expanding footprint in the AI and robotics sectors, the "turnaround" is no longer a theory; it is becoming a fundamental reality.

The Turnaround

The financial data released on April 9, 2024, tells a story of stabilization and growth. Notably, BlackBerry has notched its eighth consecutive quarter of positive GAAP net income. In a volatile tech environment, this level of consistency is a critical indicator of fiscal health.

The IoT segment is the primary engine here. According to the Q4 earnings report:

  • IoT Revenue surged 20% year-over-year in the most recent quarter.

  • Royalty Backlog stands at approximately $950 million, representing a 10% year-over-year growth.

  • QNX Achieved rule of 40 for the fiscal year.

This backlog is vital because it represents future revenue that is fairly locked in as vehicles and industrial units move into production. The legacy label often attached to BlackBerry is being dismantled by its role in the future of automation.

Secure Comms resurgence

In my prior article, I was hardest on the Secure Comms portfolio as it was lagging comparably to QNX. Secure Comms represents part of the old BlackBerry, and it seemed to be on the same path as Cylance. When your own country - Canada - wasn't fully vested in purchasing from you, it spoke volumes.

However, the tide has turned. Over the past quarter, Secure Comms expanded its partnership within Canada in a massive way. The agreement significantly increases the deployment of SecuSUITE—encrypted voice, messaging, and file-sharing—across federal departments through 2033. Showcasing that Canada is now leaning into security sovereignty and buying into it's home-grown tech.

The financial impact is real: Secure Comms revenues hit $67 million in Q3 and $72.5 million in Q4, and the company has raised its full-year Secure Comms outlook to a range of $247 million–$251 million. Seeing this segment pivot from contraction to a projected 6% growth is the stabilization metric I needed to see.

The Hidden Upside: Malikie and Arctic Wolf

For value seekers, two interesting pieces remain on the board: the Malikie Patent Deal and the Arctic Wolf Equity.

The Malikie Patent Deal: BB sold 30,000+ patents to Malikie for $200m, which consisted of a $170 million upfront payment and a $30 million payment due by May 2026. With active lawsuits against several major companies (e.g. Nintendo, Samsung, Hisense, NTT Global Data et al.), the profit-sharing agreement (ranging from 8% to 50%) could provide a non-dilutive cash windfall once Malikie is made whole. Malikie likely just reached a settlement deal with Canon (as they voluntarily dismissed their lawsuit). Malikie and Blackberry also joined the Avanci Wi-FI 6 licensing agreement that should help additional monetization of their technology.

The Arctic Wolf Equity: Arctic Wolf purchased the Cylance unit for $160M and still owes BB ~$38M (due around Feb 2026). Critically, BlackBerry holds 5.5 million shares in Arctic Wolf. With a private market "fair value" of roughly $44M ($8/share), this is a sweetener for new investors. There has been industry chatter about a late 2026 or early 2027 IPO.

My previous fair value estimate was a cautious $4.27. I’m tossing that in the trash. With QNX growing at 20%, a massive Nvidia partnership in hand, and the debt being forced into equity, my new price target is $5.12, with a high target of $7.16 and a median target of $6.06.

Cleaning up the Balance Sheet

For years, the $200 million convertible debt has been the anvil around the BlackBerry stockholder's ankles. I worried about the dilution and the logical ceiling it created for shareholders.

Well, we just hit the ceiling—and we’re breaking through it. With the stock surging on the recent Nvidia news, we’ve officially cleared the $5.04 forced redemption threshold. For BB to blow past this "ceiling" means that retail + institutional investors now want a piece of BlackBerry and aren't concerned about potential dilution.

If the price stays above an average price of $5.04 for 20 days, BlackBerry could force those noteholders to convert their debt into equity. Subsequently, I would hope BlackBerry protects their investors by retiring a part of these shares with the extra cash that was raised. The benefits of early debt retirement are:

  1. Debt vanishes as $200 million is wiped off the books.

  2. $6 million in annual interest payments goes straight back to the bottom line.

  3. BlackBerry is left with over $230 million in net cash and a clean, virtually no-debt, balance sheet.

At that time, the BlackBerry story would be even more compelling. Growth + clean balance sheet would be hard to ignore.

Mea Culpa: The Giamatteo Vision

I previously criticized Giamatteo for being too "operational" and not "visionary" enough. However, the "operational" is exactly what was needed when you're cleaning up a decade of mess. He pruned the underperforming Cylance assets and doubled down on the two things BlackBerry does better than anyone else: secure links and a safety-certified OS.

Giamatteo told the Wall Street Journal that 20% of QNX revenue now comes from outside the auto sector. That diversification into robotics and medical devices is exactly the visionary strategy I was looking for.

Valuation

I modeled various scenarios based on leadership being able to grow the business using the DCF model. I created forecasts based on BlackBerry's growth estimates, which led to a 2 percentage point increase from my prior DCF model (from 8% to 10%). The 8% discount rate was derived using a CAPM model, where I used a beta of 1.01, risk premium of 4.6%, and current risk free rates. I also took into account the amount of net cash BB has, which included the Malikie mandatory payments in cash and cash equivalents. The model is now signaling a base valuation of $5.12 with a upside of $7.16. The upside would entail quicker adoption of QNX beyond auto, continued Secure success, Arctic Wolf IPO, Malikie profit sharing.

Conclusion

BlackBerry has successfully transitioned from a period of operational cleanup to one of strategic expansion. The combination of record Q4 earnings, expansion of QNX and Secure Comms, the potential elimination of debt through the $5.04 conversion trigger, and the ultimate validation of the Nvidia partnership creates a compelling bull case.

With the IoT engine primed and the cybersecurity segment stabilizing, the company is finally hitting the strikes that investors have waited years to see. I am raising my rating to a Buy. For the first time in this turnaround story, the smart move is to bet on the fundamental strength of the new BlackBerry.

data.title: Alphabet Is Getting Ready For War
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) is preparing to publish its Q1 CY26 ER next week. But that is not the big catalyst that I believe will drive the next phase of the search giant’s transformation into a full-fledged frontier model company.

Given Alphabet’s strong earnings performance history, the company is widely anticipated to beat all expectations.

However, the market’s focus will be elsewhere. I believe markets will closely watch how Alphabet plans to navigate the enterprise AI market—an increasingly lucrative target market for almost every leading frontier model company in the world.

A recently leaked memo from Google’s founders, followed by an aggressive product roadmap announced at the Google Cloud Next26 conference last week, revealed Alphabet’s most tenacious plans to battle for a share of the enterprise AI market.

I am still neutral on Alphabet’s outlook, but I reveal below key things I'm watching from Alphabet’s Q1 ER and conference call next week.

How Alphabet Is Waging War On The Enterprise AI Market

Among the flurry of company ERs, war updates, and macro news, I believe markets overlooked some key product updates that were presented at the Google Cloud Next26 conference last week. While I maintain a transitory neutral view on Alphabet’s outlook, I believe, long-term, last week’s conference was very important in remaking the face of Alphabet from search to a frontier model company.

Three core themes from last week’s product announcements:

Firstly, the most important update was Workspace Intelligence, which unifies all the different enterprise apps (Mail, Docs, Sheets, Drive, etc.) scattered across the digital workplace into collaborative AI agents that autonomously work with one sole goal—boost an employee’s productivity. With Gemini Enterprise, Alphabet even envisions a handover of the autonomous and governance part of building and deploying AI agents completely in the hands of its enterprise customers.

Secondly, while Workspace Intelligence and Gemini Enterprise are part of Google Cloud’s product strategy to appeal to the enterprise AI market’s productivity goals, Google also launched additional agentic security capabilities in Google SecOps (Security Operations) to fire up an enterprise’s security detection and protection mechanisms against a surge of AI-driven online adversarial threats.

I believe these 2 product updates from last week are heavily tied to the vision that Google co-founder Sergey Brin has supposedly pushed for as part of a leaked memo. The same report speculates the formation of a “strike team,” of which Brin is reportedly a part of.

While the team acknowledged the progress made by Google so far, there are still some gaps that Brin is said to pressingly root for, and agentic AI capabilities are one area of strong opportunity that has been identified:

“To win the final sprint, we must urgently bridge the gap in agentic execution and turn our models into primary [code] developers”

Lastly, Google also formally announced two new TPUs—one v8 TPU for training workloads and one v8 TPU for inference workloads, exactly as I anticipated. Google says their new network architecture allows them to connect up to 1152 TPUs at a time, which is an incredible feat.

I believe these 3 strategic pillars—enterprise productivity, agentic platforms & custom chips—are part of Google’s vision to tackle the enterprise and eventually boost further growth into Google Cloud.

Impact To Google Cloud, Alphabet & Its Valuation

At the moment, markets are projecting Google Cloud to grow ~45.3% in CY26, if I factor in consensus estimates for Google Cloud’s CY26 revenues of ~$85. 3B. Here’s how that compares to the growth estimates of its hyperscaler peers.

In next week’s Q1 call, I suspect many analysts will be curious as to when exactly Google plans to see its recently launched product portfolio for the enterprise market boost growth rates of its revenues and/or its bottom lines.

After all, Google Cloud is an important part of Alphabet’s growth engine. Factoring in the forward expectations of $85.3B cloud revenues and overall top-line consensus estimates of $473.23B, Google Cloud is estimated to account for almost a fifth of Alphabet’s revenues. So I do expect the focus of the call and ER next week to be on its strategy to target the enterprise with its agentic AI tools.

The focus on Google Cloud and its new enterprise product roadmap is of paramount importance, especially when “enterprise rivalry” is heating up between Anthropic and OpenAI. Competition is due to be heating up for sure for the enterprise.

This also tees up Alphabet’s unique approach to keep investing in Anthropic, by supplying it with compute, boosting Claude processing capabilities, yet competing with Claude directly for a share of the enterprise. Bloomberg reported on Google’s plans to invest up to $40B in Anthropic which will also bring questions on Google’s strategy on investments and ROI.

Staying on the topic of investments, I expect management will raise its 2026 AI capex beyond the ~$180B projections to boost long-term growth for its Google Cloud segment and combat fierce competitive threats from formidable peers.

But management has to be very careful in articulating the case for upgrading its 2026 capex because investors will also be focusing on gross margins, potential for forward growth rate revisions, and most importantly, capital efficiency metrics like ROIC.

Heading into earnings, I am a little cautious on Alphabet’s outlook because the company does look overstretched on a forward valuation basis. Heading into Q1 earnings, shares trade at 29-30x CY26 earnings that are expected to jump just 8.2% while also trading at a forward sales multiple of 8.8x CY26 sales that is expected to jump 17.5%.

I am very intrigued by Alphabet’s product roadmap and would like to re-enter GOOG shares. But I am sitting on the sidelines because the earnings multiple is expensive for me given the high single-digit earnings growth outlook.

Moreover, Google’s Brin himself states that the team still needs to bridge the “gap in agentic execution,” acknowledging some work needs to be done to battle for the enterprise AI market. Starting next week itself, I believe the coming month or two will be critical in shaping my forward-looking views on buying some GOOG shares for my portfolio.

But for now, I will pass on the opportunity of buying GOOG shares at a forward earnings of 29-30x, especially since it has earnings next week.

What I’m Looking For Next Week in Alphabet’s Q1

  • Q1 Expectations: $2.63 EPS (down 6.4%) on revenues worth $107B (up 18.5%)

  • Q2 Expectations: $2.78 EPS (up 20%) on revenues worth $113.7B (up 17.9%)

  • CY26 Expectations: $11.7 EPS (up 8.2%) on revenues worth $473.2B (17.5%)

  • CY26 Google Cloud Expectations: $85.3B

  • CY26 Capex: $175-185B

Takeaway

I believe Alphabet is turning the page to a new era of innovation, moving away from the search business that made it an innovation icon over the past 3 decades.

Alphabet has done brilliantly so far to position itself as a leading innovation company in the age of agentic AI. However, I will watch the coming phase—the battle for the enterprise AI market—closely, as I remain neutral on Alphabet’s shares.

data.title: Chatham Lodging Trust: Why I Am Still Buying The High-Yielding Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

I have covered the preferred shares issued by Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) for several years now. And in my most recent update in October of last year, I even used a ‘strong buy’ rating on the preferred stock. I think these preferred shares still offer an attractive risk/reward ratio given the strong preferred dividend coverage and the robust balance sheet.

While I'm not necessarily a big fan of owning the common stock of hotel REITs, my position in the preferred shares of Chatham is still increasing as the 8.3% preferred dividend yield is just too good to pass up on. This hotel REIT focuses on extended stay properties, which account for about 66% of its EBITDA.

The preferred shares remain well covered, and the balance sheet remains strong

When I'm looking at preferred shares, I mainly focus on two specific elements. I, of course, want to make sure the preferred dividends are well covered by either the net income (or, in the case of REITs, the adjusted FFO), while I also want to make sure that the balance sheet is safe enough to withstand economic shocks.

As Chatham Lodging Trust is a REIT, the first step to determine how attractive the preferred stock is, is the FFO result. As there is always some seasonality in the hotel business, it's always useful to have a look at the full-year results rather than trying to extrapolate quarterly results.

As you can see below, Chatham reported a total adjusted FFO of $52.75M, and I would like to highlight here that this result already includes the just under 8 million USD spent on the preferred dividends.

This indeed means that the total adjusted FFO before the preferred dividends exceeded $60M, which indeed means Chatham Lodging needed less than 15% of its pre-distribution FFO to cover these preferred dividends. And that percentage will decrease as Chatham expects its adjusted FFO to increase by approximately 10% this year (before taking additional acquisitions into account).

The REIT has also provided guidance for 2026, and as you can see below, it expects to report an adjusted FFO of $53-58M. We also know Chatham Lodging expects to spend approximately $26M on capital expenditures this year, which includes $17M for the renovation of three hotels. This will, of course, also weigh on the full-year performance, but the REIT will remain free cash flow positive, and the recently increased dividend will be covered.

The REIT recently also announced it acquired six hotels with a combined 589 hotel rooms for a total amount of $92M. All six hotels are Hilton-branded, and were acquired at a capitalization rate of approximately 10% based on the 2025 net operating income. The impact of this acquisition was not yet included in the guidance for 2026, as shown above, so I am looking forward to seeing the management commentary after publishing the Q1 results.

Excluding these acquisitions, the pre-distribution adjusted FFO would come in at $63.5M, requiring Chatham less than 13% of that amount to cover its preferred dividends. I expect this percentage to decrease as the new properties will start to contribute from the current quarter on.

Not a whole lot has changed on the balance sheet front since my previous article, so I’d like to refer you back to my October 2025 publication. I will provide a balance sheet-related update after the REIT publishes its Q1 results, as the updated quarterly statement will include the impact of the newly acquired hotel properties.

The details of the Series A preferred stock

The REIT only has one series of preferred stock (CLDT.PR.A) outstanding, and these preferred shares are currently trading at $19.86. This represents a discount of in excess of 20% to the $25 call price. This means the current yield exceeds 8.3%.

These cumulative preferred shares can be redeemed from June 30 this year on, but it is unlikely Chatham will call these preferred shares, as 6.625% still is a reasonable cost of capital for the entity. But I would, of course, be totally fine should I be wrong. If Chatham effectively calls the preferred stock, that would generate an immediate 25%+ gain from the current share price.

I am investing in these preferred shares based on the perspective that I will receive an 8%+ preferred dividend return for the next few years, and I am definitely not banking on the preferred shares getting called.

Investment thesis

While I'm starting to warm up to owning a long position in the common units of this hotel REIT, I have now reached an overweight position in its preferred stock. I think the market is wrong in its perception of the risk/reward ratio associated with an investment in these preferred shares. Considering the post-distribution adjusted FFO will come in at approximately $55.5M (the midpoint of this year’s guidance), resulting in a post-capex free cash flow of around $30M, I consider the preferred dividends to be very safe. Even if the FFO would drop by 50% (which I don’t anticipate happening), the preferred dividends and the capex would still be covered. Consensus estimates are putting the FY 2027 FFO at $1.26 per share, representing in excess of $60M.

I continue to nibble on the preferred shares whenever they trade below $20.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: AI Means Business, And The Valuation Isn't Low Enough Yet
data.description:
Cybersecurity stocks have gotten caught up in the software selloff. Shares of Palo Alto Networks (PANW) plunged nearly 40% from their October 2025 high to the “Citrini Research” February 2026 low under $140. Ahead of earnings due out in May, I reiterate a hold rating.

I was neutral on the stock back in April 2024. Shares have returned 23% since then, but that is about half the S&P 500’s return. Today, I see the valuation as still fair, while the technical situation puts the onus on the bulls amid a seemingly shoot-first, ask-questions-later environment for software names.

I’ll offer a fresh look at PANW’s fundamentals, valuation, and technicals.

PANW Lags the Broader Market YoY, Outperforming the BUG Industry ETF

Back in February, PANW reported a solid set of quarterly results. Its Q2 non-GAAP EPS of $1.03 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.94, while revenue of $2.59 billion, up 15% from the same period a year earlier, was a small $10 million beat. The double-beat was paired with a lukewarm outlook, though, and the stock dropped the following day.

Indeed, PANW plunged 6.8% in the session that followed, its second straight post-earnings decline. Looking ahead to the May 20 Q3 report, the options market prices in a slightly below average 6.4% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Implied volatility on the $145 billion market cap Software industry stock is elevated at 51%, and short interest has ticked up to 3.3%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, PANW posted decent Q2 numbers, with significant growth across its core platforms and continued momentum in its "platformization" strategy. Total revenue rose by mid-teens double digits YoY, while the company’s Next-Generation Security (NGS) Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) grew by 33% to $6.3 billion.

PANW tagged a non-GAAP operating margin of 30.3% and a non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.03, representing 27% growth compared to the prior year. Operating margins expanded by 200 basis points due to improved efficiencies, and management updated its fiscal year guidance to incorporate the strategic acquisitions of CyberArk and Chronosphere.

Key operational highlights included surpassing 1,550 total platformizations, with notable expansion in SASE ARR (up about 40%) and the Cortex XSIAM platform, which exceeded $500 million in ARR.

Color on Quarter

Looking ahead to the full-year 2026, Palo Alto sees total revenue between $11.28 billion and $11.31 billion and NGS ARR between $8.52 billion and $8.62 billion. It reaffirmed a long-term adjusted free cash flow margin target of 40% for FY'28 in its Q2 report.

More broadly, the back half of the year appears significantly de-risked due to a combination of conservative organic targets and inorganic contributions from recent acquisitions.

2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to rise by 10.5% this year, with a slowdown seen in the out year. By FY 2028, the company may experience higher bottom-line growth, but sentiment is clearly on edge. That assertion is buttressed by a staggering number of sell-side EPS downgrades.

There have been 45 negative revisions in the past 90 days, compared to just a trio of upgrades. Still, PANW’s profitability trends are strong, and free cash flow per share is up to $5.23 over the past 12 months.

PANW: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $3.80 of normalized non-GAAP EPS over the next 12 months and apply a mid-40s price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $170. Given AI’s threat to the cybersecurity industry, I don’t think a near-60 P/E is justified (as that would also be about a 5x PEG ratio, based on a low-teens long-term EPS growth rate).

Even with the recent stock price decline, PANW still trades above its long-run price-to-sales ratio, too.

PANW: Premium Valuation Multiples

Key risks for PANW include concerns about AI's potential to disrupt traditional security systems, growth in competition from the likes of CrowdStrike (CRWD), and risks surrounding M&A in the fast-changing industry.

There have also been increases in the firm’s cost of goods sold, possibly reflecting supply chain hiccups—that bears watching as the year progresses.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q3 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, May 20 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With a still-premium valuation and looming threats from advancing AI, PANW’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares have been putting in a bearish rounded topping pattern, highlighted by the October 2025 high. Key support has emerged near $140, and a break below that would indicate extreme AI penetration realities in the cybersecurity industry. What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is now on the decline, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. It’s not all bad news, though.

Take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It’s solidly on the rise, with a series of higher highs and higher lows. Thus, momentum confirms the thrust off support. I also noticed that PANW did a successful re-test of the February low back in March, and that low was a bullish abandoned doji candlestick. The February nadir also came on very high volume, indicating a capitulation low.

Still, the onus is on the bulls to lift PANW through the 200dma and a high amount of volume by price up to near the $200 mark.

PANW: Bearish Rounded Top, Falling 200dma, $140 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on PANW. Shares have lagged the broader market in the past two years, and even the major cybersecurity and software selloff since last October does not leave the stock all that attractive on valuation.

data.title: AMD Bulls Went Too Far, Too Fast
data.description:
Over the past few quarters, the shares of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) struggled to form a decisive trend in either direction. The stock spent a lot of time trading below the 50DMA (blue line) on three occasions, and the distance between the 50DMA and 200DMA (orange) started narrowing more recently.

Of course, the contrast between the post-earnings selloff this year and the recent surge raises questions.

For starters, I guess the post-earnings selloff makes sense. The Q4 FY2025 results were absolutely good, but the market expected more. Revenue reached $10.3 billion, up 34% YoY and 11% QoQ. AMD also beat the LSEG consensus by about 6.2%. However, QoQ growth in Q3 was 20%, indicating a significant deceleration. Gross margin was at 54%, up 2 percentage points QoQ. Operating income surged 101% YoY to $1.75 billion, also reflecting a substantial 38% QoQ growth. Net income also surged to $1.51 billion, marking a 213% and 22% YoY and QoQ increase, respectively. Diluted EPS reached $0.92, a 217% YoY growth and 23% up, sequentially. Again, a significant deceleration has taken place since Q3 net income and diluted EPS increased by 43% and 39% QoQ, respectively. Free cash flow broke the pattern as it increased to $2.08 billion, which was 91% higher than the same quarter last year and 36% up sequentially, versus the QoQ growth of 30% in Q3.

The transition from a very strong quarter is even more painfully visible once you break down the revenue. Data Center, which as of the last report contributes 52.4% of the total revenue, increased by 39% YoY to $5.38 billion. This is the segment that the market is most likely focused on due to the AI factor. Therefore, the QoQ growth of 23.9%, which is meaningfully lower than the 34% QoQ increase in the third quarter, disappointed investors.

Client and Gaming added more fuel to the slower pace narrative because it was responsible for 38.4% of total sales, and, though it reached $3.94 billion (reflecting a 37% YoY growth), it fell by 2.7% sequentially (versus +11.8% QoQ in the third quarter). What we have here is a mature segment being a drag on the AI boom story. Client revenue wasn't the issue; it increased by 12.6% QoQ. Gaming, at 8.2% of total revenue, was the source of the drag, decreasing by 35.1% sequentially.

Before I continue, the takeaway from this is not that the last quarter was unusually weak; that is far from what happened. It just followed an unusually strong quarter. As opposed to the market back then, I see nothing fundamentally alarming about the Q4 results.

Now, what seems to have kept AMD shares trading within the 50-200 DMA channel after the initial market reaction to earnings is the macro backdrop, which investors often ignore. The war in Iran and subsequent Hormuz blockage raised the price of oil, which in turn elevated near-term inflation expectations. The last FOMC meeting confirmed the fear of making any hard-to-reverse errors when it came to monetary policy, as the Fed kept rates steady and updated the SEP to reflect the increased likelihood of higher inflation. AMD behaves like a long-duration asset, and the lower likelihood of near-term relief from decreasing yields is consistent with that performance following the selloff. The market needed to see a positive shift in the macroeconomic outlook before it grew more bullish on tech stocks.

This macro backdrop apparently contributed to the market being strangely numb to some very positive catalysts. First, on February 24, AMD signed the Meta (META) AI deal, involving a multi-year deployment of Instinct GPUs, EPYC CPUs, ROCm, and AMD Helios. On March 18, the company also signed an MoU with Samsung, under which the latter becomes the primary HBM4 supplier for AMD's MI455X AI accelerator and provides advanced DDR5 memory optimized for AMD's 6th-gen EPYC CPUs and Helios system.

Then we got the "AI broadens beyond GPUs" narrative becoming more prominent. On March 25, Arm's (ARM) AI CPU launch served as a sign that agentic AI and inference will be greatly supportive of CPU names like AMD. It also signaled that AMD doesn't need to outcompete Nvidia (NVDA) on GPUs for the market to grow optimistic about the stock. The "relative market position" question mark has been an important part of the narrative that may have weighed on AMD shares.

Of course, the idiosyncratic factor of AMD positioning itself for more spending on AI infrastructure is credible here. Both the Meta agreement and the Samsung MoU hint at the company's understanding of the growth potential and reflect its justifiably proactive approach. The sector-level confirmation we got from ASML and TSMC forecasts back on April 16 is also indicative of that growth potential that the management team sees.

So what's the issue? Well, we got two promising signals that the market found inadequate to steer AMD into a new major uptrend until early April, when it gained meaningful distance from the 50DMA for the first time. The market reaction went from being underwhelming to overwhelming quite fast. Month-to-date, AMD is up 71%, and surged by roughly 14% on Friday following Intel's (INTC) strong revenue outlook. Such price action naturally raises the question about whether the current level is sustainable.

AMD guided Q1 revenue to ~$9.8B, implying a 5% sequential decline. It also guided for non-GAAP gross margin at 55%, a 2 percentage point implied decline from Q4's non-GAAP 57%. The next earnings report, scheduled for May 5, is now especially important because the burden of proof has shifted, and a merely solid quarter is unlikely to be enough.

One issue is that in Q4, AMD got a boost from MI308 China revenue of $390 million; excluding that contribution, Data Center would have delivered $4.99 billion, below the estimated $5.07 billion. AMD expects the contribution from MI308 sales to China to be significantly lower this time, at ~$100 million. Q4 clearly benefited from a one-off contribution, and investors would scrutinize how AMD offsets the lower scale of such a favorable condition this time. The point is that even if the company beats guidance, a sticky point is whether it does it by a hair with the help of as volatile an item as China sales. After the market closes on May 5, I know this is what I'm going to check first.

The second point of attention should be on the non-GAAP gross margin because it's expected to decline. However, due to the latest price performance, the market may have to digest that decline all over again. Though the AI narrative now includes CPUs, AMD still competes with Nvidia, so the margin differential is unlikely to stop being another sticky point for the market so quickly. The expected margin compression will test the degree to which the bulls have evolved to ignore this differential.

Now, as the CPU/inference narrative is what is driving the current market optimism, investors should look for any signs that validate the narrative, specifically with AMD. Obviously, guidance should be significantly raised, but I would look for any signs that CPU revenue is accelerating. Data Center revenue will be scrutinized by investors, and anything less than the 23.9% sequential increase realized in Q4 is unlikely to preserve the current momentum in price performance, especially given the recent price surge, which can only be justified if it priced in an AI-related CPU sales growth acceleration. Since Data Center revenue is not broken down further in the results, investors will also have to rely on management commentary regarding EPYC sales growth. In the last earnings call, the CEO said,

In server, adoption of fifth-gen EPYC Turin CPUs accelerated in the quarter, accounting for more than half of the total server revenue. Fourth-gen EPYC sales were also robust as our prior generation CPUs continue to deliver superior performance and TCO compared to competitive offerings across a wide range of workloads. As a result, we had record server CPU sales to both cloud and enterprise customers in the quarter and exited the year with record share.

So, color that is any less promising than that in the next call would be an issue, given that the high expectations are driven by the CPU's more prominent role going forward.

Since the Meta AI deal is a meaningful part of the longer-term bull case, in the earnings call, I would also look out for any comments about that first gigawatt deployment, scheduled to start in the second half of 2026. Anything related to the degree of confidence about the timing and scale could help investors check if the recent repricing, probably anchored to the Meta deal to some degree, is warranted. Similarly, another point of interest should be the MoU with Samsung. Even if it turns out that any development there doesn't move the needle for the market right now, progress would at least help investors better gauge the health of the growth potential, as it also depends on memory supply security and system optimization. Since the MoU mentions a possible foundry partnership, any agreement there would also be beneficial, as AMD would gain more manufacturing flexibility.

The current valuation leaves no margin for disappointment. The forward earnings multiple of 45x is meaningfully above the 5-year average of 37x, and the forward PEG is almost the same as the 5-year average ratio.

Moreover, the technicals indicate overbought conditions. After the price climbed back above the 50DMA, it wasn't long before it started trading above the upper Bollinger band; the channel is set at 2.5σ, which highlights that the move has gotten statistically stretched. That is not just strong momentum. It is a violent rerating prompted by catalysts that haven't yet received confirmation by something as substantial as AMD's guidance. The 14-day RSI is also extraordinarily high at ~89; the only time it reached that level was back in 2003.

All in all, there is no margin for error anymore. My base case is a post-earnings pullback as results/guidance expected early next month fail to justify the magnitude of the rerating; that would create an opportunity for current shareholders to buy more shares. My bull scenario is that the stock holds its ground at this level and, at worst, goes into a consolidation phase after earnings before it attempts to climb higher; that is currently too much to ask for, and if it happens, depending on the report, it may offer a good entry point for those who have faith in the long-term potential. Bear case: AMD prints in line with guidance and doesn't significantly raise guidance yet; the price may start testing the 200DMA again, which would imply a great opportunity for both traders and investors.

Of course, I find the bear scenario as unlikely as the bull one. But the base case suggests that entry at this level before the next report is released is not warranted. The most reasonable course of action for non-shareholders is to wait for confirmation that the earnings call at least matches the market's vibe. For a shareholder, trimming would also make sense at this level.

I want to make clear that the longer-term bullish outlook for AMD, as discussed above, is solid. I have no major objections regarding the case for CPU names such as this when it comes to the AI buildout theme transitioning into a new phase. Nor do I think that AMD is not well-positioned to take advantage of this transition. But the bulls went ahead and moved the price too far, too fast, ahead of confirmation that the rerating matches what AMD sees in the near term.

Was this article helpful? Let me know below. If you disagree, feel free to explain why. I enjoy challenging my thinking. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Tech Heavyweights To Report Next Week, Fed Rate Decision Also In Focus
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon. U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting the ambassadors of both countries in Washington. On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April-to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March-according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

Investors will be looking forward to the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision next week, which will be out on Wednesday. The consumer confidence data, along with housing numbers, is scheduled for Tuesday. On Thursday, jobs data and the quarterly GDP numbers will be released. S&P Global and ISM PMI data are scheduled for Friday.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday: Verizon (VZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Visa (V), Coca-Cola (KO), Starbucks (SBUX), British American Tobacco (BTI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), AbbVie (ABBV), Qualcomm (QCOM), Ford (F). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Apple (AAPL), Eli Lilly (LLY), Merck (MRK), Mastercard (MA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Pitney Bowes (PBI), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Skillz (SKLZ). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Northrop Grumman (NOC), Lockheed (LMT), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). Short interest is elevated on SoundHound AI (SOUN) and BigBear.ai Holdings (BBAI) again.

IPO watch: Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman's investing vehicle, plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst's quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Cal-Maine (CALM), AGNC Invest (AGNC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Costco (COST).

Apple Earnings Preview: Apple is expected to report second-quarter earnings of $1.95 per share on revenue of $109.69B next week. Analysts expect the company to maintain its momentum driven by robust iPhone demand in China and India, despite supply chain constraints and chip cost pressures. In Q2 2025, the gross margin is estimated to reach between 48-49%, up from 48.16% in Q1 2026. The earnings commentary is likely to revolve around the company's most recent announcement, where Apple named John Ternus as Tim Cook's successor. Investors are likely to be keenly looking out for the management's further comments on it. On the AI front, analysts at BofA Global Research see Apple's M5 family as a meaningful step towards the company's AI compute stack, given that M5 is AI-optimized to run workloads like diffusion models on devices. "AAPL looks relatively insulated from the AI-related volatility," they added.

Alphabet Earnings Preview: Alphabet is projected to post Q1 EPS of $2.63 and revenue of $106.93B. Ahead of the earnings, analysts expect a short-term volatility due to elevated CapEx, market expectations, and potential pressure on free cash flow. Alphabet Inc. has already outlined its substantial capital expenditure plans for the year, and the first quarter should offer an early glimpse into that investment ramp-up. They have also expressed concerns over the rising popularity of Gemini potentially cannibalising its major revenue generator, Google Search. Despite the concerns, a solid Q1 print is expected, especially on the back of Google Cloud. "Even if CapEx is a delicate issue, if this (Google Cloud) business division maintains strong top-line growth, with healthy margins and a strong backlog, it will be an excellent signal that the business is sustainable and that demand is real and consistent," according to a Seeking Alpha analyst.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street’s major averages ended the week mixed, even as the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped to new highs as the conflict in the Middle East continued to lack resolution while oil prices rose.

Earlier this week, U.S. President Donald Trump said the U.S. would extend its ceasefire with Iran until a coordinated plan for an end to the war, which would include Israel, was brought forward. However, he has left the ongoing blockade in place.

He also accused Iran of violating a ceasefire agreement after Iranian forces fired upon ships in the Strait of Hormuz, including a French vessel and a British freighter. Later, Trump ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz with “no hesitation."

Iran has said it will not participate in a second round of talks with the U.S., objecting to what it described as excessive and unrealistic demands from Washington. Also, Iran’s parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, has reportedly stepped down as Tehran’s lead negotiator.

Lastly, Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting the ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) increased around 13.2% this week to settle around $94, and Brent futures (CO1:COM) also rose 17.2% to $105 per barrel as of post-market Friday.

In economic news, retail sales jumped more than expected in March, while February business inventories rose +0.4% MoM to $2,686.8B vs. +0.2% consensus, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Also, the AAII survey showed that bullish sentiment among individual investors surged, while bearish views declined. Lastly, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) gained +0.6%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose +1.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) declined -0.4%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.


Importers vs. consumers

Customs and Border Protection launched a tariff refund portal this week, with thousands of companies rushing to file claims for the duties they paid over the past year. It's unclear how many of them will pass the benefit on to consumers, but retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) stand to receive billions in refunds, while the tech, media and telecom industry could get over $47B. Read more.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Berkshire (BRK.B): Abel Arbitrage, Strong Returns Post-Buffett.

Star Bulk's (SBLK) Updated Dividend Policy, Macro Tailwinds.

Trump's Green Light - Cannabis Stocks (MSOS) Are Surging.

The Worst Is Now Over, Here's Why It's Time To Buy FSCO.

Orion Properties (ONL): Refinancing Risk Is Off The Table.

Enterprise Products (EPD) May See Limited Upside In 2026.

Enbridge (ENB): Moving To The Sidelines After A Great Run.

Locking In Gains - Sell Amazon (AMZN) On Anthropic Spike.

Campbell's (CPB) Is A Sell Amid Troubling Core Inefficiencies.

Here's Why It's Time To Move On From Coterra Energy (CTRA).

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.4% to 49,231. S&P 500 +0.6% to 7,165. Nasdaq +1.5% to 24,837. Russell 2000 +0.4% to 2,787. CBOE Volatility Index +7% to 18.71.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples +1.2%. Utilities +0.1%. Financials -1.9%. Telecom -0.8%. Healthcare -3.1%. Industrials -0.6%. Information Technology +3.1%. Materials +0.1%. Energy +3.2%. Consumer Discretionary -0.3%. Real Estate -1.4%.

World Indices
London -2.7% to 10,379. France -3.2% to 8,158. Germany -2.3% to 24,129. Japan +2.1% to 59,716. China +0.7% to 4,080. Hong Kong -0.7% to 25,978. India -2.3% to 76,681.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI +12.6% to $94.4/bbl. Gold -2.8% to $4,740.9/oz. Natural Gas -5.7% to 2.523. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 4.31.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD -0.36%. USD/JPY +0.47%. GBP/USD +0.12%. Bitcoin +2.6%. Litecoin +1.8%. Ethereum -1.4%. XRP +0.3%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +25%. United Rentals (URI) +22%. Texas Instruments (TXN) +21%. Intel (INTC) +21%. ON Semiconductor (ON) +19%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
Charter Communications (CHTR) -24%. Tractor Supply (TSCO) -18%. lululemon athletica (LULU) -14%. Northrop Grumman (NOC) -14%. Lockheed Martin (LMT) -13%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: First Industrial Realty: A Stress-Free REIT With Reliable Growth Drivers
data.description:
Industrial real estate has gone through a reset over the past two years. Demand cooled from pandemic highs, supply briefly surged, and investors started asking harder questions about durability. Think of REITs like Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), whose valuation has fallen on hard times as a result.

But beneath that noise, a handful of operators have kept executing with discipline. First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) is one of them, and its latest results suggest the story is shifting back toward steady growth paired with reliable income.

I last covered FR back in February 2025, highlighting its solid operating fundamentals. Since then, FR has delivered a respectable 15.4% total return. In this article, I revisit the stock and discuss why it’s a ‘Buy’ on strong execution, so let’s get started!

A Focused Industrial Platform With Embedded Growth

First Industrial Realty Trust is a pure-play industrial REIT, owning and developing logistics properties across key U.S. markets. Its portfolio is built around distribution hubs, infill locations, and development-ready land. These are assets that benefit from long-term trends like e-commerce, supply chain modernization, and domestic manufacturing.

As shown below, its markets are strategically centered around supply-constrained Tier 1 and growing Sunbelt markets.

FR’s strategy has been consistent for years. It prioritizes spec development in high-demand markets, maintains a flexible land bank, and rotates capital when values are compelling. That approach is now paying off in multiple ways.

One of the most notable recent developments is a pending $131 million land sale in Phoenix’s 303 corridor. The pricing, about $30 per land square foot, represents more than three times prevailing industrial land values in that market.

This is not just a one-off gain. It highlights the embedded value within the company’s land portfolio and its ability to monetize assets opportunistically.

Earnings Reflect Strength Beneath the Surface

First Industrial’s first-quarter results were steady on the surface but stronger when you look underneath.

Funds from operations came in at $0.68 per share, flat year over year. Adjusted for one-time advisory costs tied to a proxy contest, FFO would have been $0.72 per share. More importantly, operating fundamentals showed clear momentum.

Cash same-store NOI grew 8.7%, driven by higher rental rates, reduced concessions, and contractual rent bumps. Leasing spreads remain robust, with overall cash rental rate increases hitting 41% on new and renewal leases. That figure was boosted by a major Southern California renewal. But even excluding that, rent growth remains well above historical norms.

Occupancy held firm at 94.3%, and FR has already addressed 61% of its 2026 lease expirations. That early progress reduces rollover risk and locks in higher rents ahead of time.

Industry Tailwinds Are Stabilizing And Turning Positive

The broader industrial backdrop is beginning to fall in favor again. National vacancy held steady at 6.7% in the first quarter. Net absorption reached 43 million square feet, only modestly trailing new supply. Meanwhile, construction starts remain muted, and the development pipeline is highly pre-leased at 39%.

The combination of steady demand, slowing supply, and disciplined development sets the stage for tightening fundamentals over time. FR is already seeing early signs of this shift. Touring activity by prospective tenants is increasing, and decision-making is accelerating, particularly for spaces under 200,000 square feet.

Importantly, management does not see geopolitical risks, including Middle East tensions, as having a measurable impact on leasing so far. That stability reinforces the resilience of the underlying demand drivers.

Full-year FFO guidance remains in the range of $3.09 to $3.19 per share, excluding one-time costs. Same-store NOI growth is expected at 5% to 6%, with occupancy projected between 94% and 95%.

There is also a subtle but important shift underway. As supply growth slows and demand stabilizes, pricing power is holding. Rent growth in Southern California has flattened sequentially but remains positive, and long-term trends still support further increases.

Financial Strength and Disciplined Capital Allocation

FR maintains a solid BBB-rated balance sheet with a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 5.0x. This supports the primary growth engine of speculative development, which typically generates higher returns than acquisitions. Historically, about 20% to 25% of capital has gone toward acquiring stabilized properties, with the majority directed toward development.

At the same time, FR is increasingly willing to recycle capital. The Phoenix land sale is a prime example, in which it monetized a non-income-producing asset at an attractive valuation and redeployed proceeds elsewhere.

FR currently yields a respectable 3.3%, and the dividend is well covered by a 64% payout ratio. It also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 12.5% and 12 consecutive years of growth.

FR doesn’t appear to be cheap on the surface at the current price of $61.37 with a forward P/FFO of 19.5. However, I believe the valuation is well-justified considering FR’s strong cash leasing trend and forward outlook. I believe FR is positioned to grow FFO per share in the high single-digit range over the medium term. This, combined with the dividend yield, could lead to total returns in the low teens annually.

Risks to Watch

Leasing velocity for large spaces continues to lag smaller footprints. Decision-making could remain slow in that segment, delaying occupancy gains. Supply, while moderating, is still working through the system in certain markets. Areas like the Inland Empire have elevated availability in mid-sized spaces, which could pressure rents in the near term.

There are also execution risks tied to development. Timing, lease-up pace, and construction costs all matter, particularly for speculative projects. Plus, while data center-related demand is growing, it is not yet a major driver. If broader industrial demand weakens, that segment alone would not be enough to offset it.

Investor Takeaway

First Industrial Realty is not a flashy story. It is a steady one, and that is exactly the appeal. It’s combining strong leasing economics, disciplined development, and opportunistic capital recycling to drive both income and growth. Rent spreads remain elevated, occupancy is stable, and embedded land value provides additional upside.

At the same time, industry conditions are shifting back in its favor. Supply is moderating, and demand is broad-based, supporting FR’s pricing power. For investors, this creates a compelling long-term setup.

Investors are getting a REIT with reliable cash flow and visible rent growth, with multiple levers for value creation. In the current market that often swings between extremes, that kind of consistency is worth paying attention to for sleep-well-at-night returns. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on FR.

data.title: Selling The Furniture To Pay The Rent: The Unsustainable Reality Of Strategy, Inc. Preferred
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

We don't usually write bearish articles. We find that it is usually much more constructive to talk about the things we want to buy as opposed to talking about the much larger number of things that we choose not to buy.

However, there is a group of investments that members have been frequently asking about, and it is an investment vehicle that is as terrible an idea as "CDO squared" back in 2008. Unfortunately, it is created specifically to appeal to the greed of income investors.

We are talking about several issues of preferred securities issued by Strategy Inc. (MSTR). We love preferred equities, and we own a lot of them at High Dividend Opportunities. We have a preference for preferred equities that have a higher than average yield and are with companies outside of the traditional banks and insurance companies that dominate the preferred asset class. So naturally, some members are looking at the high yields of the MSTR preferred shares and think they look great.

We don't own any, we would not add them to the model portfolio, and we strongly urge anyone considering buying these to completely understand the risk they are taking.

What Is MSTR?

MSTR is a software company that has a modest and slightly profitable software business. They started buying Bitcoin (BTC), and today, their Bitcoin holdings dwarf their software business. MSTR now describes itself as a Bitcoin Treasury Company. Which in Plain English means "We own a bunch of Bitcoin so you should buy our stock". Because that's it, they own and hold Bitcoin and have a little software side business.

MSTR owns a lot of Bitcoin. As of December 31st 2025, they owned $58.8 billion in Bitcoin. Source

Plus about $2.3 billion in cash, which makes up substantially all of their assets. What does MSTR do with this Bitcoin? Nothing. They just hold it and buy more.

MSTR's revenues come from product licenses, subscription services, and product support – totaling about $477 million last year. Their gross profit in 2025 was just $327.8 million, or about 0.5% return on their assets.

That's before they pay for marketing, R&D, and G&A. In other words, against the backdrop of their Bitcoin portfolio which declined in value by $5.4 billion last year, their revenues are quite insignificant.

What MSTR Is Doing Today

MSTR seems to have little interest in the software portion of the company. Instead, they are focused on buying as much Bitcoin as they can. With minimal organic cash flow from the company, MSTR is buying Bitcoin by raising equity. It has been issuing common equity and now preferred equity in massive amounts.

Most recently, MSTR issued $1.0028 billion of the preferred STRC, which was used to acquire $1 billion worth of Bitcoin. In essence, MSTR funded a purchase of Bitcoin purely with capital raised from the preferred.

MSTR has authorization to issue massive amounts of equity, primarily through $21.6 billion of STRC and $27.1 billion in common equity.

Here is our estimate of MSTR's outstanding preferred after this issuance based on their dashboard at Strategy.com:

They have 104 million preferred shares outstanding, and they are paying cash dividends of $277.97 million per quarter, or about $1.11 billion/year.

Remember, their annual gross revenue had ranged from $463 million to $496 million over the past three years. MSTR does not have the cash flow to even pay for one quarter of dividends from its operating business.

How Is MSTR Paying The Preferred?

With no recurring cash flow, MSTR can only pay for the preferred dividends in one of three ways:

  • Utilizing cash on hand. MSTR has approximately $2.25 billion in cash according to their dashboard at Strategy.com. That's enough to pay dividends on the current preferred for about two years.
  • Issuing more equity. MSTR can issue either common or preferred equity to raise cash. This can help with immediate liquidity, but in the case of preferred issuance, it leads to higher dividend obligations in the future, and it dilutes the common if they go that route.

  • Selling Bitcoin. MSTR has $60.4 billion in bitcoin at current prices. Their average cost of Bitcoin is $59 billion, so they have a small gain at today's prices. The problem with that is that MSTR then has less Bitcoin and would need the price to go up faster to continue paying the preferred. This works great if Bitcoin only goes up in price.

So, MSTR is issuing preferred equity like it is going out of style, and its plan is to either keep issuing equity or to sell its assets to pay the dividends. This would be a wonderful plan if you knew that Bitcoin is going to be $100,000 next year.

If Bitcoin is say $50,000, then to pay the $1.1 billion in dividends for the year, MSTR would need to sell 22k Bitcoin. It then has a smaller base from which to pay the $1.1 billion the following year. Every sale, MSTR realizes permanent capital losses, and the amount that needs to be sold to honor the preferred dividends increases. A year or two is probably manageable. If Bitcoin goes down in price and stays down, it's unsustainable.

In other words, preferred shareholders need Bitcoin to go up in price in order for their dividends to be sustained. As MSTR issues more preferred equity, the higher the risk to the previously existing preferred. MSTR is showing no indication of slowing down; it is prudent to assume that it's going to continue issuing equity and buying more Bitcoin. And the amount owed for the annual dividend will increase.

STRC

It's worth noting that the preferred that MSTR is issuing the most of is STRC, which has a variable dividend. STRC's dividend is adjusted in an attempt to keep the price trading at $100. So if the price drops, the dividend will be raised so that investors buy more of it.

That sounds great, right? If it becomes less popular, the price drops, and you'll get a dividend raise!

It is important to note that this is not guaranteed. From the prospectus:

"Our current intention, which is subject to change in our sole and absolute discretion, is to adjust the regular dividend rate in such a manner as we believe will maintain STRC Stock’s trading price at or close to its stated amount of $100 per share."

In other words, it is their "intention", and they can change it whenever they want, and there isn't a thing you can do about it. It's a gimmick, and it's designed to help MSTR continue to issue as much STRC as possible near $100/share. It reminds us of the "non-traded" preferred issued by a REIT, Ashford Hospitality Trust (AHT), which had provisions that allowed for preferred shareholders to demand redemption for cash or common equity at par value after a period of time. This incentivized shareholders to buy because they thought they could wait out the waiting period and then just demand redemption at par and be "guaranteed" to get their money back.

Well, they ended up with too many preferred outstanding, and decided to suspend redemptions and the dividends.

We aren't predicting that the same will happen with MSTR, but it certainly is something that can happen. MSTR can stop increasing the dividend and let the price of STRC crash whenever it wants to, and it can suspend the dividends.

For now, MSTR wants to issue more shares, so keeping the price elevated is worth the extra costs. The day MSTR decides it doesn't need or want to issue more shares, the variable dividend is going to get reduced. If Bitcoin prices are low, it could be suspended completely.

Conclusion

The bottom line is that MSTR's preferred shares rely on Bitcoin going up in price. It might. Maybe Bitcoin is headed to $1 million. I've seen people make that claim. Our point is that for these investments, it is an affirmative bet that Bitcoin is going to go up in price at a fairly steady pace. If it doesn't, then the preferreds are not going to collect all their dividends, and they aren't going to recover par. MSTR is placing a big bet on Bitcoin using other people's money, and if you are buying the preferred, it's your money that is being bet with. You're taking the risk on Bitcoin, but if it does go up, you're only getting a small piece of the reward. If it goes down, you're losing your money as assuredly as if you just bought Bitcoin yourself.

If you are going to take on the risk of Bitcoin prices, you should make sure you are also getting the potential reward if Bitcoin continues to go up and outperform. These preferred take on the risk, but pass along a large portion of the reward to the common shareholders.

Invest in preferred stock with companies that have recurring cash flow to cover their distributions. If you want to take a portion of those dividends and put it in Bitcoin, great.

This type of financial engineering doesn't end well in my experience. Buying a preferred equity that is entirely dependent upon issuing new equity or liquidating the asset base isn't a good idea. CDO Squared worked great as long as housing prices only went up. MSTR preferred work great as long as Bitcoin only goes up.
